metadata.summary: Core message queue topologies: point-to-point work queue, publish/subscribe fan-out, request/reply, and dead letter queue. Covers when each pattern applies and how to compose them.

content.title: Message Queue Patterns
content.summary: Message queues enable asynchronous communication between services, providing decoupling, load leveling, and reliability. Four foundational topologies cover the vast majority of use cases: point-to-point work queue, publish/subscribe fan-out, request/reply, and dead letter queue for failed messages.

why.p: Synchronous request/response couples the caller and callee: if the email service is down, the order service also fails. Message queues break this coupling. The producer publishes and returns immediately; one or more consumers process the message when they are ready. This unlocks independent scaling, failure isolation, and load leveling during traffic spikes.

Choosing the wrong topology introduces subtle correctness bugs. Fan-out without considering consumer independence leads to ordering conflicts; request/reply through queues adds unnecessary latency when a direct synchronous call would suffice.

list.item: Decoupling services in distributed systems so failures isolate — the order service should not block when the email service is down.
Async background jobs (image resize, ETL, ML inference) where users should not wait on the response path.
Load leveling — absorb traffic spikes that the downstream cannot handle synchronously.
Event-driven architectures with multiple consumers (audit, analytics, search index) per published event.
Reliable hand-off across process or network boundaries with at-least-once or exactly-once delivery semantics.
Request/response with low latency requirement under 50 ms end-to-end — synchronous gRPC or HTTP wins.
Strong-consistency transactional flows that span multiple services — use SAGA or 2PC instead, but understand the trade-offs first.
Adding queues just to scale without measuring backpressure or queue depth — adds operational burden with no benefit.
Cache invalidation chains — use pub/sub on Redis directly.
Pure fan-out logging — use a log shipper instead.
Tiny apps that fit on one box — a goroutine with a channel or a Python queue.Queue is simpler and faster.

section.name: core-topologies
section.p: The four canonical topologies and their routing diagrams:

example.title: Point-to-Point (Work Queue)
example.code: Producer → Queue → Consumer (one consumer processes each message; competing consumers share load)
example.note: Default choice for background jobs. Each message is processed exactly once across all competing consumers.

example.title: Publish/Subscribe (Fan-out)
example.code: Producer → Exchange → Queue1 → Consumer1 → Queue2 → Consumer2 → Queue3 → Consumer3
example.note: Use when one event must reach multiple independent consumers (audit, analytics, search index). Each consumer gets its own queue; failure in one consumer does not affect others.

example.title: Request/Reply
example.code: Client → Request Queue → Server Client ← Reply Queue ← Server (correlation_id links reply to original request)
example.note: Async RPC over queues. Each client creates a temporary reply queue or uses a shared reply queue filtered by correlation_id. Adds latency vs. direct HTTP — use only when the server-side work is genuinely async.

example.title: Dead Letter Queue (DLQ)
example.code: Producer → Main Queue → Consumer ↓ (failed after max retries) DLQ → Error Handler / Manual Replay
example.note: Poison messages are isolated rather than retried forever. The DLQ must be monitored and drained; without alerting it becomes a silent graveyard.

rule.statement: Before writing any queue code, state the topology explicitly in a contract document: queue name, schema, partition key, ordering requirement, retry policy, DLQ target, and idempotency strategy. The contract is the primary deliverable; code is secondary.
rule.rationale: Topology decisions have long tails. A schema change or retry-policy change in production requires explicit reviewer approval. Writing the contract first surfaces these decisions while they are cheap to change.

rule.statement: Select the topology based on the consumer relationship: one message, one consumer (work queue); one message, many independent consumers (fan-out); async call expecting a reply (request/reply); all others routed after max failures (DLQ as complement to any of the above).
rule.rationale: Mixing topologies without explicit intent leads to messages processed by the wrong consumer set or correlation IDs lost between services.

antipattern.description: Using request/reply queues for latency-sensitive operations under 50 ms, such as authentication token validation or read-heavy API lookups.
antipattern.reason: Queue round-trip adds broker serialization latency on top of network latency. For sub-50 ms requirements, direct synchronous gRPC or HTTP is the correct choice.

antipattern.description: Fan-out without per-consumer queues — broadcasting to a single shared queue that all consumers read from.
antipattern.reason: Competing consumers on a shared queue produce work-queue semantics, not fan-out. Each consumer gets only a fraction of messages. Use a broker-level exchange (RabbitMQ topic/fanout) or SNS topic to create one queue per consumer group.

antipattern.description: Missing correlation IDs — publishing messages without a stable trace identifier across services.
antipattern.reason: Without a correlation ID, a message cannot be traced through the system when it fails. Debugging becomes a log-grepping exercise across multiple services with no join key.

rule.statement: Design every consumer for idempotency. Messages may be delivered more than once under at-least-once delivery semantics; the consumer must produce the same side-effect on the first and every subsequent delivery of the same message.
rule.rationale: No major broker guarantees exactly-once delivery without application-level cooperation. Building idempotency into the consumer is cheaper and more reliable than trusting broker-level deduplication.

rule.statement: Every queue that can receive a failure must have a DLQ configured before the queue goes live. DLQ depth must be wired to an alert.
rule.rationale: Without a DLQ, failed messages are either lost or retry forever. A DLQ without an alert is equivalent to no DLQ — messages accumulate silently and business logic stops working.

rule.statement: Monitor queue depth, consumer lag (Kafka), DLQ size, oldest-unacked-age, and processing error rate. These metrics are the earliest incident signals for distributed systems.
rule.rationale: A growing queue depth indicates the consumer cannot keep up. DLQ growth indicates systematic failures. Without these metrics, incidents surface only after user-visible impact.

checklist.check: Topology named and justified (work queue / fan-out / request-reply / DLQ complement).
Message schema defined in a registry (Avro, Protobuf, or JSON Schema) with version.
Partition key or routing key chosen based on ordering requirements.
Retry policy set: max retries, backoff strategy, jitter.
DLQ configured and alert on DLQ depth wired.
Idempotency strategy documented (idempotency key field, dedup store).
Monitoring: queue depth, lag, DLQ size, oldest-unacked-age, error rate.
